heroOverview: Paragonimiasis is a foodborne lung fluke infection caused chiefly by Paragonimus westermani and related species — trematodes that migrate from the intestine through the peritoneum and diaphragm to establish in the lung parenchyma or, less often, in the brain, skin, or abdominal organs. The WHO estimates approximately 20 million people are infected worldwide, with hundreds of millions at risk in East and Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America. Humans acquire infection by eating raw or undercooked freshwater crustaceans — crabs and crayfish — that carry infective metacercariae. Established pulmonary infection produces a chronic cough with rust-coloured or blood-streaked sputum, chest pain, and breathlessness that closely mimics pulmonary tuberculosis; the two conditions frequently co-exist in the same endemic populations. Praziquantel is the first-line treatment and is highly effective.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 20-22 million people infected globally (WHO estimate); 290 million at risk
keyStats.demographics: Predominates in East and Southeast Asia (especially Korea, Japan, China, Philippines, Vietnam) and parts of West Africa and Latin America; affects all ages but young adults and children who eat raw crustaceans are at highest risk
keyStats.avgAge: No strict age cut-off; cases cluster in communities where raw crab or crayfish consumption is culturally common
keyStats.globalCases: More than 50 Paragonimus species described; P. westermani causes the most cases globally; P. kellicotti is the main North American species

definition: Paragonimiasis (ICD-10: B66.4) is a zoonotic trematode infection caused by Paragonimus lung flukes, of which Paragonimus westermani is the most prevalent human pathogen. The life cycle involves two intermediate hosts: freshwater snails (first intermediate host) and freshwater crabs or crayfish (second intermediate host). Humans and other carnivores (cats, dogs, wild felids) are definitive hosts who become infected when they eat raw, pickled, or inadequately cooked crustaceans containing metacercariae. After excystation in the small intestine, juvenile flukes penetrate the intestinal wall, migrate through the peritoneum, diaphragm, and pleural space to reach the lung parenchyma, where they mature into adult flukes inside fibrous cysts near the bronchioles. The disease is classified as a neglected tropical disease by WHO and falls under infectious disease and pulmonology. The classic clinical syndrome is pulmonary paragonimiasis presenting as a chronic cough with haemoptysis that is radiologically and symptomatically indistinguishable from pulmonary tuberculosis. Ectopic paragonimiasis occurs when flukes migrate to the brain (cerebral paragonimiasis), abdomen, skin, or spinal cord rather than completing pulmonary migration.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; flukes establish in fibrous cysts in the lung, causing chronic cough, blood-streaked or rust-coloured sputum, chest pain, and patchy infiltrates on imaging — a picture that closely mimics tuberculosis.
Migration of flukes to the brain, forming space-occupying granulomas or cystic lesions; presents with headache, focal seizures, motor deficits, and can be fatal. More common in children.
Flukes abort migration and establish in the abdominal cavity or walls, producing tender nodules, ascites, and right-lower-quadrant pain that may mimic appendicitis.
Ectopic migration to the subcutaneous tissue produces migratory, painless, nodular swellings, most often on the trunk or extremities.

primarySymptoms: Chronic cough lasting weeks to months — typically productive of rust-coloured, brownish, or blood-streaked sputum; often the presenting complaint and the symptom that most strongly mimics pulmonary tuberculosis.

Haemoptysis — coughing up frank blood or heavily blood-streaked sputum, occurring in up to 50-80% of pulmonary cases and reflecting erosion of bronchiolar blood vessels by migrating or established flukes.
Pleuritic chest pain — a sharp, positional pain worsening on deep breathing, arising from pleural inflammation as larvae cross the diaphragm and pleural space.
Breathlessness — progressive dyspnoea on exertion in patients with extensive bilateral lung involvement or accompanying pleural effusion.
Fever — low-grade, intermittent fever during the acute migratory phase; less prominent in established chronic pulmonary infection.
Night sweats — particularly in patients with concurrent tuberculosis, adding to diagnostic confusion.
Fatigue and malaise — common throughout the illness, reflecting the chronic inflammatory burden.
Abdominal pain — colicky or diffuse pain during the initial peritoneal migratory phase, before the flukes reach the lung.
Urticaria and pruritus — cutaneous immune responses during acute larval migration.
Headache, focal seizures, or visual disturbance — neurological symptoms when flukes migrate to the brain (cerebral paragonimiasis), which is the most dangerous form.
earlyWarningSigns: A new cough beginning 2-6 weeks after eating raw crabs or crayfish in an endemic region.
Mild haemoptysis in a young person without a prior history of respiratory disease in East or Southeast Asia.
Blood eosinophilia on a routine blood test in someone with a cough and relevant food exposure.
Low-grade fever with chest discomfort following travel to or residence in a paragonimiasis-endemic country.
emergencySigns: Massive haemoptysis — coughing up more than 100-200 mL of blood in 24 hours, which can be life-threatening and requires emergency airway management.
Sudden severe headache, focal neurological deficits, or seizures in a patient with pulmonary paragonimiasis, suggesting cerebral migration or cerebral granuloma rupture.
Signs of tension pneumothorax or large pleural effusion: sudden worsening breathlessness, deviated trachea, and absent breath sounds.
Acute abdomen with peritoneal signs during the early abdominal migratory phase.

causes.cause: Eating raw or undercooked freshwater crabs or crayfish
causes.description: The primary and almost exclusive route of infection. Freshwater crabs and crayfish harbour metacercariae in their muscle and hepatopancreas. Raw, pickled, or minimally marinated preparations (such as Korean 'gejang' or Vietnamese 'cua rang muoi') deliver viable larvae that excyst in the human duodenum.
Cutting boards and utensils used to prepare infected raw crustaceans can contaminate other foods with metacercariae, extending transmission beyond direct crustacean consumption.

causes.cause: Consuming wild boar or other paratenic hosts
causes.description: In some species such as Paragonimus kellicotti and P. skrjabini, metacercariae can migrate to paratenic hosts — wild pigs, rats — and remain infective; humans who eat raw paratenic host muscle acquire infection.

causes.cause: Drinking water containing juvenile flukes from crushed crabs
causes.description: In some regions, crabs are crushed to extract juice used as a folk remedy or food ingredient; this juice can contain viable metacercariae that infect consumers who drink it.

causes.cause: Environmental amplification through snail and crustacean habitats
causes.description: The first intermediate host (freshwater snails, especially Semisulcospira and Brotia species) must be present in fast-flowing streams. High snail infection rates, combined with abundant crab populations, sustain persistent human exposure in endemic watershed communities.

riskFactors.factor: Cultural consumption of raw or lightly prepared freshwater crustaceans
riskFactors.description: Traditional dishes using raw, pickled, or wine-marinated freshwater crabs are the dominant risk factor in Korean, Chinese, and Taiwanese populations. Behavioural modification — cooking crustaceans above 60°C for 5 minutes — completely prevents infection.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to East or Southeast Asia
riskFactors.description: Paragonimiasis is hyperendemic in parts of South Korea, southern China, northern Vietnam, the Philippines, and western Japan; travellers who adopt local food practices are at risk.
People who catch and immediately consume freshwater crabs and crayfish from endemic streams have high exposure frequency and often lack access to cooking facilities in the field.
Children represent a disproportionate share of cerebral paragonimiasis cases, possibly because immature immune responses allow more ectopic migration.

TB and paragonimiasis are epidemiologically linked in the same resource-limited endemic regions; the two infections overlap clinically and each can mask the other, delaying correct diagnosis and appropriate treatment.

HIV infection and other immunosuppressive states impair the eosinophil and T-helper 2 immune response that normally limits fluke migration, potentially enabling heavier worm burdens and more frequent ectopic disease.

affectedDemographics: Rural communities in South Korea, China, and the Philippines where traditional raw crab consumption is common
Young adults and adolescents in endemic areas who participate in communal feasts involving raw crustaceans
Children in endemic regions, who are at greatest risk of cerebral paragonimiasis
West African communities consuming raw freshwater crabs (Potamonautes species) in Cameroon, Nigeria, and Democratic Republic of Congo
Latin American populations in Peru, Ecuador, and Central America where Paragonimus mexicanus and related species are endemic
Travellers and expatriates who eat local raw-crab dishes in endemic countries
diagnosisOverview: Paragonimiasis must be actively considered in any patient with chronic haemoptysis or pulmonary infiltrates from an endemic region, because its presentation is clinically indistinguishable from pulmonary tuberculosis; misdiagnosis results in delayed effective treatment and unnecessary anti-TB therapy. Definitive diagnosis relies on identifying characteristic operculated eggs (85-90 µm long, with a distinct shouldered operculum) in sputum, bronchoalveolar lavage, or stool — sputum examination is positive in roughly 40-60% of pulmonary cases, and stool microscopy reflects swallowed sputum eggs. Serological testing with ELISA for Paragonimus-specific antibodies achieves sensitivity of 90-96% and is particularly valuable in the acute phase and in ectopic disease where eggs may not be detectable. Blood eosinophilia is typically present (15-40%), particularly in the early migratory phase. Chest X-ray and CT characteristically show bilateral ring-shaped or cystic lesions, patchy infiltrates, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax, often with a 'worm track' infiltrate. Brain CT or MRI in cerebral cases shows hypodense cysts, calcified lesions in chronic disease, or ring-enhancing granulomas. ERCP is not relevant in paragonimiasis (unlike fascioliasis). Sputum AFB smears and TB culture must be performed in parallel because co-infection occurs in 3-8% of cases in high-burden settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly detects the characteristic large, operculated eggs in expectorated sputum, confirming active pulmonary infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You produce a deep-cough sputum sample, ideally an early-morning specimen, into a sterile container. Multiple samples (three on consecutive mornings) are requested to improve sensitivity. Results take 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects eggs swallowed with infected sputum; particularly useful in patients who swallow rather than expectorate their sputum.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is collected at home and examined by concentration microscopy in the laboratory. Sensitivity is approximately 30-45%, lower than sputum examination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects specific IgG antibodies against Paragonimus antigens; highly sensitive (90-96%) and particularly important in ectopic disease, early infection, and where sputum/stool examination is negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results from a reference laboratory take several days. Antibodies may persist for months after cure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies eosinophilia (typically 15-40% of white cells) that supports a parasitic rather than a bacterial or primary malignant cause of pulmonary symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with results in a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the distribution and morphology of pulmonary lesions — ring shadows, cystic lesions, linear track opacities, pleural effusion — to distinguish paragonimiasis from TB, malignancy, and other causes of haemoptysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Chest X-ray takes minutes; CT requires lying in the scanner for 10-20 minutes and may involve contrast. Images are reviewed by a radiologist.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI or CT (in neurological cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cerebral granulomas, cystic lesions, calcifications, or ring-enhancing lesions in cerebral paragonimiasis; guides treatment and monitors response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30-60 minutes in a noisy, enclosed magnet; CT is faster (5-10 minutes) and more widely available. Brain imaging is reserved for patients with neurological symptoms.

treatmentOverview: Praziquantel is the first-line treatment for all forms of paragonimiasis and is highly effective. The standard WHO regimen is 25 mg/kg three times daily for 2 consecutive days (total 150 mg/kg over 48 hours), administered with meals. Cure rates exceed 90% for pulmonary disease confirmed by egg clearance at 1-3 months. Triclabendazole at 10 mg/kg twice in one day is an effective alternative, with equivalent cure rates reported in several trials and particular value in areas where praziquantel resistance has been documented. Bithionol, an older drug, is largely abandoned due to side effects and inferior cure rates. In cerebral paragonimiasis, praziquantel is still first-line treatment, but corticosteroids are co-administered to reduce the inflammatory reaction around dying flukes in the brain, which can temporarily worsen symptoms or provoke seizures if not controlled. Antiepileptic drugs are prescribed for seizures and may need to be continued for 12-24 months. Surgical excision of cerebral granulomas is reserved for refractory cases with raised intracranial pressure or obstructive hydrocephalus. Pulmonary complications such as chronic pleural effusion or destroyed-lung segments may require pleural drainage or, rarely, surgical decortication. Follow-up sputum and serology are assessed at 3 and 6 months to confirm cure. Concurrent tuberculosis, when confirmed, must be treated with a full standard anti-TB regimen in parallel; treating paragonimiasis alone without addressing TB does not improve long-term pulmonary outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anthelmintic; increases cell membrane permeability to calcium, causing spastic paralysis of fluke musculature. Given as 25 mg/kg three times daily with meals for 2 days. Well tolerated; side effects include transient dizziness, abdominal discomfort, and headache.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure confirmed by egg clearance in approximately 90-95% of pulmonary cases after one 2-day course.

medicalTreatments.name: Triclabendazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative first-line agent at 10 mg/kg twice in one day. Particularly useful when praziquantel is unavailable or where reduced susceptibility is suspected. Works by disrupting fluke microtubule assembly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85-95% reported in comparative trials; equivalent to praziquantel in most settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (for cerebral paragonimiasis)
medicalTreatments.description: Corticosteroids (typically dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg/day) are co-administered with praziquantel in cerebral disease to suppress the inflammatory reaction around dying intracranial flukes, reducing the risk of seizures and cerebral oedema during treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces treatment-related neurological deterioration; approximately 70-80% of cerebral cases show clinical and radiological improvement within 3-6 months of combined therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic therapy (for seizure control in cerebral cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard antiepileptic drugs (carbamazepine, valproate, or levetiracetam) are used for seizure control in cerebral paragonimiasis and continued for 12-24 months or until lesions resolve on imaging.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure freedom achieved in the majority of patients with well-controlled drug levels; most patients can be weaned off antiepileptics after lesion resolution.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision of cerebral granuloma
surgicalOptions.description: Craniotomy and excision or aspiration of refractory cerebral granulomas causing mass effect, obstructive hydrocephalus, or uncontrolled seizures despite drug therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reserved for a small minority of cases; success rates for lesion removal exceed 80% in specialist neurosurgical centres, though praziquantel remains the mainstay of treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cooking all freshwater crustaceans
alternativeTreatments.description: The most effective prevention strategy: cooking crabs and crayfish at temperatures above 60°C for at least 5 minutes destroys all metacercariae. This single behaviour change eliminates the transmission route.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support
alternativeTreatments.description: High-calorie, protein-rich diets support immune function and lung recovery during and after treatment; supplementation with iron may be warranted if blood loss from haemoptysis has caused anaemia.

whySeeSpecialist: Paragonimiasis requires an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist to access praziquantel or triclabendazole, which are not standard formulary drugs in most non-endemic countries. A pulmonologist should be involved when the diagnosis is being differentiated from tuberculosis, malignancy, or other causes of haemoptysis, and when pleural complications arise. Cerebral paragonimiasis requires a neurologist and neurosurgeon experienced in neuroinfectious disease, and the decision to add corticosteroids and antiepileptics needs specialist oversight.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist with experience in foodborne trematodes who can order and interpret Paragonimus serology and co-ordinate access to appropriate drug treatment. For pulmonary complications, a pulmonologist with bronchoscopy capability is valuable. Cerebral cases require co-management with a neurologist and, if surgery is being considered, a neurosurgeon with neuro-oncology or CNS infection experience.
preventionStrategies: Cook freshwater crabs and crayfish thoroughly (above 60°C for at least 5 minutes) before eating — this is the single most effective preventive measure.
Do not eat raw, pickled, marinated, or insufficiently processed freshwater crustaceans in any endemic region.
Avoid drinking water or juice derived from crushing raw freshwater crabs.
Use separate cutting boards and utensils for raw crustaceans, and wash surfaces thoroughly with hot water and soap after preparation.
Educate fishing and foraging communities about the link between raw crab consumption and chronic haemoptysis.
Screen individuals with chronic haemoptysis in endemic areas for paragonimiasis before initiating anti-TB therapy, to avoid misdiagnosis and unnecessary treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Adopt cooking practices that ensure all freshwater crustaceans are cooked through; adjust traditional recipes to use boiling or steaming.
Inform family members who share meals about the infection and recommend evaluation if they consumed the same raw crab preparation.
Avoid smoking, which compounds pulmonary damage in patients with paragonimiasis.
Rest and limit strenuous activity during the acute migratory phase and early treatment when symptoms are most active.
Attend all follow-up appointments for sputum checks and serology to confirm cure.

recommended: Thoroughly cooked freshwater crustaceans — boiled, steamed, or fried at sufficient temperatures
High-protein foods (legumes, eggs, lean meat) to support immune function and tissue repair
Iron-rich foods or supplements if haemoptysis has caused iron-deficiency anaemia
Adequate hydration to facilitate sputum expectoration
avoid: Raw, pickled, marinated, or lightly salted freshwater crabs and crayfish in any form
Freshwater crab juice used as a food ingredient or folk remedy
Smoking, which worsens pulmonary inflammation and slows recovery
Alcohol, which may compound general malnutrition in endemic areas and interfere with drug tolerability

exerciseGuidelines: Strenuous aerobic activity should be limited during active pulmonary disease and haemoptysis, as increased ventilatory effort can provoke further bleeding or discomfort. After completing praziquantel therapy and confirming reduced haemoptysis — typically within 2-4 weeks — gradual return to normal physical activity is encouraged. Patients with cerebral paragonimiasis should avoid contact sports and activities with seizure risk until neurological stability is confirmed.
dailyManagement: Complete the full 2-day praziquantel course, taking all doses with meals to minimise gastrointestinal side effects.
Report any worsening haemoptysis, seizures, or new neurological symptoms immediately — these may indicate treatment-related inflammatory reactions requiring corticosteroid cover.
Return for sputum examination and serology at 1-3 months and again at 6 months to confirm parasitological cure.
Ensure TB co-infection has been explicitly excluded or is being treated in parallel.
Alert household contacts who shared the implicated raw crab meal so they can be assessed.
prognosis: The prognosis for pulmonary paragonimiasis treated promptly with praziquantel is excellent: over 90% of patients achieve parasitological cure, haemoptysis resolves within weeks, and pulmonary infiltrates clear over 3-6 months. Chronic untreated infection leads to progressive lung destruction, bronchiectasis, and respiratory failure over years in heavily infected individuals. Cerebral paragonimiasis carries greater morbidity: approximately 70-80% of patients improve with praziquantel and corticosteroids, but residual neurological deficits, epilepsy, and calcified lesions persist in a subset. Mortality from cerebral paragonimiasis is uncommon with modern treatment but does occur in cases diagnosed late with mass effect or in resource-limited settings. Co-infection with tuberculosis worsens the respiratory prognosis if both conditions are not treated concurrently. With adequate diagnosis and treatment, paragonimiasis is curable and long-term respiratory or neurological sequelae are the exception rather than the rule.
recoveryTimeline: Haemoptysis typically lessens within 2-4 weeks of completing praziquantel. Sputum egg clearance and serological decline are confirmed at 1-3 months. Chest imaging usually shows marked improvement by 3-6 months, with complete resolution by 12 months in most cases. Cerebral lesions take 6-24 months to regress radiologically, and antiepileptic drugs are maintained until imaging confirms resolution.
complications: Bronchiectasis — permanent dilation and scarring of airways after years of untreated or recurrent pulmonary infection, causing chronic productive cough and susceptibility to secondary bacterial pneumonia.
Pneumothorax and pleural effusion — direct inflammatory involvement of the pleura by migrating flukes, requiring drainage in significant cases.
Cerebral paragonimiasis — fluke migration to the brain causing seizures, focal deficits, hydrocephalus, and rarely death; the most serious complication of the disease.
Secondary bacterial pneumonia — superimposed bacterial infection in damaged lung tissue, particularly with Streptococcus pneumoniae or gram-negatives.
Misdiagnosis as pulmonary tuberculosis — initiating anti-TB treatment without diagnosing paragonimiasis leads to prolonged ineffective treatment and unnecessary drug toxicity.
Pulmonary fibrosis — long-standing bilateral parenchymal inflammation can result in restrictive lung disease and reduced exercise tolerance even after parasitological cure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosomiasis causes urinary or bowel symptoms and hepatic/portal pathology depending on species, not pulmonary haemoptysis; acquired from contaminated water rather than crustacean consumption.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fascioliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fascioliasis targets the liver and bile ducts rather than the lung, is acquired from raw aquatic plants not crustaceans, and presents with right-upper-quadrant pain and jaundice rather than haemoptysis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary echinococcosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary hydatid cyst produces a smooth, round cystic lesion on imaging and rarely haemoptysis; serology for Echinococcus is distinct from Paragonimus serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Strongyloides larvae cause a transient Löffler-like pulmonary syndrome during larval migration, not chronic haemoptysis; larvae are detected in stool rather than sputum eggs.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another foodborne trematode causing significant organ pathology; shares anthelmintic treatment options including triclabendazole and praziquantel.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A biliary trematode infection also transmitted through raw freshwater food; part of the same WHO foodborne trematode disease group.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A trematode infection endemic in overlapping geographic regions that can cause pulmonary hypertension in its hepatosplenic form.

relatedConditions.name: Echinococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A cestode causing cystic lung lesions that radiologically resemble paragonimiasis cysts.

faqs.question: What is paragonimiasis?
faqs.answer: Paragonimiasis is a parasitic infection caused by Paragonimus lung flukes. People acquire it by eating raw or undercooked freshwater crabs or crayfish. The flukes migrate to the lungs and cause a chronic cough with blood-streaked sputum that closely resembles tuberculosis.

faqs.question: How is paragonimiasis transmitted?
faqs.answer: Paragonimiasis is transmitted almost exclusively by eating raw, pickled, or undercooked freshwater crabs and crayfish that carry infective larvae. The infection is not spread person to person. Cutting boards contaminated with raw crustacean juices can also transfer larvae to other foods.

faqs.question: How does paragonimiasis differ from tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Both conditions cause chronic cough, haemoptysis, and lung infiltrates. Key differences: paragonimiasis serology is positive for Paragonimus antibodies, blood eosinophils are elevated (15-40%), sputum eggs are present, and sputum AFB cultures are negative. TB has a history of TB exposure and responds to anti-TB drugs, not praziquantel.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of paragonimiasis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are a chronic cough producing rust-coloured or blood-streaked sputum, pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, and low-grade fever. In the early phase there may be abdominal pain and urticaria. Severe headache and seizures point to cerebral paragonimiasis, the most dangerous form.

faqs.question: What drug treats paragonimiasis?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel is the first-line treatment: 25 mg/kg three times daily with meals for 2 days. Cure rates exceed 90%. Triclabendazole (10 mg/kg twice in one day) is an effective alternative. Cerebral cases also require corticosteroids to reduce brain inflammation during treatment.

faqs.question: How is paragonimiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis relies on finding Paragonimus eggs in sputum or stool, or by blood serology showing specific antibodies. Elevated blood eosinophils support the diagnosis. Chest CT shows ring-shaped cystic lesions or pleural effusion. Brain MRI or CT is used when neurological symptoms are present.

faqs.question: Is paragonimiasis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Paragonimiasis cannot spread from person to person. The parasite requires freshwater snails and crustaceans to complete its life cycle, so human-to-human transmission does not occur through coughing, contact, or shared utensils.

faqs.question: Where in the world is paragonimiasis found?
faqs.answer: About 20 million people are infected worldwide. The disease is most prevalent in East and Southeast Asia (South Korea, China, Philippines, Vietnam, Japan) and also occurs in West Africa (Cameroon, Nigeria) and Latin America (Peru, Ecuador, Mexico). Cases appear elsewhere in travellers who ate raw crustaceans abroad.

faqs.question: Can paragonimiasis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cerebral paragonimiasis occurs when larvae migrate to the brain rather than the lungs, causing granulomas, seizures, headaches, and focal neurological deficits. It is more common in children and is the most dangerous form of the infection. Treatment combines praziquantel with corticosteroids and often antiepileptic drugs.

faqs.question: What does the blood test for paragonimiasis show?
faqs.answer: A blood test typically shows elevated eosinophils (15-40% of white cells) and a positive Paragonimus-specific ELISA antibody test. These two findings together in someone with chronic haemoptysis and crustacean exposure strongly support the diagnosis. Antibody levels decline after successful treatment.

faqs.question: Can paragonimiasis cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: In most treated cases the lungs recover well. Long-standing untreated infection can cause bronchiectasis — permanent airway dilation — and pulmonary fibrosis, which reduce exercise tolerance and increase susceptibility to bacterial infections. Early treatment prevents these complications in the great majority of patients.

faqs.question: Is praziquantel safe?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel is generally well tolerated. Common side effects include mild abdominal discomfort, headache, dizziness, and nausea, usually transient and settling within 24 hours. It is used cautiously in cerebral paragonimiasis where dying flukes can provoke inflammation; corticosteroids are given alongside it in that setting to prevent worsening neurological symptoms.

faqs.question: How long does paragonimiasis last without treatment?
faqs.answer: Untreated paragonimiasis persists for years because adult flukes survive in fibrous cysts in the lung for up to 20 years in some reports. Chronic haemoptysis and progressive lung damage accumulate over time. Spontaneous cure is uncommon; treatment is strongly recommended whenever the diagnosis is confirmed.

faqs.question: Can I prevent paragonimiasis by freezing crabs?
faqs.answer: Standard household freezer temperatures do not reliably kill Paragonimus metacercariae. Only thorough cooking at temperatures above 60°C for at least 5 minutes consistently destroys the larvae. Pickling, marinating in vinegar, and salting are also insufficient to render crustaceans safe.

faqs.question: How soon do symptoms appear after eating infected crabs?
faqs.answer: Initial symptoms — abdominal pain, urticaria — may begin within 2-4 weeks during the migratory phase. Pulmonary symptoms, including cough and haemoptysis, typically develop 2-3 months after infection, when flukes establish in the lung tissue. Neurological symptoms in cerebral cases can appear weeks to months after initial exposure.

faqs.question: Does paragonimiasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Reinfection can occur if raw crustacean consumption continues after cure, since treatment does not provide immunity. Reinfection is the main reason for recurrent positive serology in endemic communities. Sustained behavioural change to cook all freshwater crustaceans is the only way to prevent repeated infection.

faqs.question: Why does paragonimiasis cause blood in the sputum?
faqs.answer: Adult flukes living in fibrous cysts near the bronchioles erode adjacent blood vessels and bronchial walls over time. This causes intermittent bleeding into the airways, producing the characteristic rust-coloured or blood-streaked sputum that is the hallmark of established pulmonary paragonimiasis.

faqs.question: Can paragonimiasis affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes, and children are at particular risk of cerebral paragonimiasis. In East Asian countries, school-age children participate in communal crab-eating traditions and have higher exposure rates than adults in some communities. Children with unexplained haemoptysis or seizures in endemic areas should be evaluated for paragonimiasis.

faqs.question: How is paragonimiasis treated in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel is not recommended during the first trimester; treatment is generally deferred until after delivery in the first trimester if the infection is not immediately life-threatening. In the second and third trimesters, the risk-benefit balance generally favours treatment under specialist guidance, especially for cerebral or severe pulmonary disease.

metaDescription: Paragonimiasis is a lung fluke infection from raw crabs causing chronic cough and haemoptysis. Learn diagnosis, praziquantel treatment, and prevention.
